column3: I have read the information sheet, I am 18 years of age or older, and I agree to participate.
column6: What is your current occupational status?
column7: Have you ever experienced, or do you currently struggle with any of the following? (Select all that apply)
column8: Have you ever used any tools for mood-tracking or journaling?
column9: If you answered "Yes", which specific tool(s) did you use? (e.g., Daylio, Notion, Paper Journal, etc.)
column10: I am motivated to track my mood during particularly stressful periods or when I feel my mental health is suffering (e.g., periods of anxiety or depression)
column11: I use mood-tracking apps because I want to facilitate self-awareness and reflect on my past emotional experiences from a new perspective.
column12: I use mood tracking to identify specific triggers—such as certain people, environments, or physical health issues—that impact my emotional well-being.
column13: I started using a mood tracker because of recommendations from social connections, such as friends, family members, or online communities.
column14: I use mood-tracking apps because a professional (such as a therapist or psychiatrist) recommended them as an alternative to traditional journaling.
column15: I use mood tracking as a personal resolution or as a way to document my year in a 'aesthetic ' or creative way.
column16: Ultimately, I use mood-tracking tools because I have a desire to improve my current situation and become happier overall.
column17: Are there any other reasons why you use a mood tracking tool that weren't mentioned above?
column18: I tend to avoid using mood-tracking apps during difficult moments because I dislike seeing my struggles or negative moods visualized.
column19: When I am feeling particularly down, I find the effort required to open an app and log my distress to be too overwhelming.
column20: I find mood trackers frustrating when they simply confirm I feel poorly without offering specific suggestions or solutions on how to improve.
column21: I often feel that I lack the analytical skills necessary to interpret the complex graphs and charts provided by mood-tracking apps.
column22: I feel that recording my mood only once a day is insufficient because my emotions fluctuate significantly throughout the day.
column23: I feel that using simple numbers or emojis is a 'robotic' way to track mood that fails to capture the true complexity of human feelings.
column24: I find that monthly summaries (like pie charts) often feel 'wrong' or unrepresentative because they erase the specific nuances of my individual days.
column25: The manual effort required to enter data every day is the main reason I track my mood infrequently or stop using these apps altogether.
column26: What are the main reasons why you have never used a mood-tracking app? (Select all that apply)
column27: I would be more interested in tracking my mood if I didn't have to manually input data every day.]
column28: I feel that my mood changes too much during the day to be captured by a single daily entry.
column29: I find traditional digital journals/diaries intimidating or boring.
column30: I would be more likely to track my mood if the app collected data automatically via smartwatch (heart rate, stress levels) instead of requiring manual input.
column31: I would be willing to record short daily videos if an AI could analyze my emotional state without me having to write anything.
column32: I would be concerned about sharing video or biometric data for mood-tracking purposes.

column17: Our brains are wired to remember negative emotions, so having evidence of my good days helps my overall positive outlook.
I also just like seeing the stats and it helps me keep track of certain things

Tracking physical health, sleep, etc and that impact on other areas of my life. Track patterns to challenge my view of myself (e.g. am I really a person who volunteers often or do I just tell myself I volunteer often? I can then review my tracker to see when I volunteered, did random acts of kindness, etc to review the reality). Keep a recorded timeline of events to be able to revisit later with a new perspective and therefore recalibrate the stories I tell to myself or about my past, or learn from, etc.
